sentences.text: Maria.
Yes, that's exactly what she said when she came to see me in Prague.
And she said, I want you to use my flat as your home.
I want you to come to Celakovice whenever you can.
And it'll be your second home and you'll always be welcome.
And she had such a wonderful, bright, sunny nature, and such a warm heart.

And it was so wonderful to get out of the drab flat that auntie and I were living in-- or should I call it the drab room-- and have a couple of days also away from her, that I spent very many happy weekends with Maria.

And when she told me one night that the sheets I was sleeping in between were bought and chosen by my mother, and the towel I was drying myself in were also from her, it made it all the more precious.
In fact, I never went back again to Prague without having something or other that was bought my mother in my little overnight bag.
I [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] really [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] loved those-- those weekends.
Again, I kept feeling so much closer to my parents.
My sister followed a few months after me.
But she only stayed a couple of months.

She just could not take it, could not take the country as it was, the harshness of it, of the post-war conditions, and also could not cope with the void the death of our parents had left, which was very, very hard to bear.
And she returned back to Britain shortly before the coup d'etat, shortly before the communists-- communists took over.
But I mustn't make it sound-- sound too negative, because there were many very kind and good people there who were instrumental in my rehabilitation, in a sense.
And I made some wonderful, wonderful friends.
There were very few family members that survived.
My grandmother actually died when the Gestapo arrested Auntie Bertha and took her into prison.
And that was prior to her going to the camps.
Grandmother-- the shock of it killed grandmother.
Grandfather died in Theresienstadt.
He went deranged through the conditions which were there, and which he had to meet at such an old age.
[SIGHS] And all the other relatives, of course, had similar fates.
There was one cousin, my cousin, who survived, who was 10 years older than I-- Heda.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Heda Bloch-- was called, the maiden name.
And then she was called Heda Margolius.
She married her sweetheart after the war, who also survived.
And he was an eminent lawyer.
Well, the Nazis didn't get him, but the Communist government executed him.
So she lost her husband under the communists.
So she didn't have a very good time of it either.
And then there was a distant cousin of my fathers, who found me through my book when it was-- when my autobiography was published in the States.
And she'd been looking for me vainly for years and years.
And she was a survivor.
So, you know, there weren't many of us.
And there were-- we were a very, very, large family, as most Jewish families were.
But I wanted to just tell you, very briefly, about what repercussions these-- housing-- housing us child refugees with the war societies had.
There was one young lad from our school, a year younger than me, so he was barely 16, if that.
And he was thrown into a hostel like this.
And his name was Joe, Joe [?
Placek. ?] And he was-- he was a very good friend of mine.
And he turned up at Auntie's store one night.
He was unshaven.
He was dirty.
He was hungry.
And he was a bit drunk.
We couldn't believe it.
I was so shocked.
But these people, you see-- they thought they were doing him a favor.
They gave him-- they treated him like one of them.
And Auntie was horrified to see him in that state.
So of course, she fed him.
Then she took all his clothes off of him, and put an old track suit of mine on him, and told me to take him to the communal baths while she washed all her floors.
And she said, you can stay the night.
And he stayed the night and remained with us for a whole month.
And then he disappeared.
And I never heard from him since.
And I never heard of him till many years later, when I learned that he made his way to Moravia with another man-- well, another boy from our school.
Again, another orphan who came Slovakia, actually-- and there they were taught to fly.
There was some-- and I think it was-- it was a Jewish-- I don't know what it was.
But there was some commission there sent from Israel.
But any way, they were taught to fly.
And they came-- then they went to Israel on one of the ships and fought in the Israeli war.
And they more or less founded the Israeli Air Force, and trained the pilots, and became incredible, incredible names in Israel Air Force.
In fact, Joe Placek, who then took on the name Joe Alon-- he was the Air Attache for Israel in Washington and was assassinated some years back.
And there's a museum dedicated to him.
And you know-- and only about a year ago, I was in a restaurant in England.
And next to me there were a couple of guys who were obvious Israeli pilots, flying from Israel here.
And so I started talking to them.
And I said-- I asked them if they, by any chance, knew Joe.
They said, know him?
He's a legend.
And he taught us all we know.
And I felt very proud.
And I've digressed a bit, but I thought it was important little story.
So when the communists took over-- or rather, before they took over-- I was offered a job of being trained to be a secretary to the military attache in Washington an-- or in London.
And I jumped at it.
I was, by then, studying at [?
Brock ?] University, at Charles University.
And I took on this job only a month before the coup d'etat, before the communist takeover.
During that month, I was in charge of confidential information, which came to the Ministry of Defense-- I was working in the Ministry of Defense-- from France and from England.
And there was a translation pool.
And I marked the articles and that which were to be translated, you know, and handed them out.
Well of course, after the coup d'etat, you can imagine, I was an anti-communist, pro-British, of bourgeois origin, and commun-- and Jewish, to boot.
I was very much a persona non grata.
Moreover, I never learned to keep my mouth shut, because I grew up in free England.
And I opened it far too often and far too clearly.
And unknown to me, of course, our room was bugged.
My office was bugged.
And before I knew where I was, I found myself being interrogated and held for about three days and three nights.
And I didn't know what it was about.
Before they did that, I had a-- I befriended an American businessmen who traveled through Europe on occasions.
And he wanted to marry me, but I didn't want to marry him.
And whenever he came to Prague, you know, we had dinner.
And I helped him, if he bought anything, to parcel the things up and that.
And when I was interrogated, they knew my every move.
They had my every letter over the last three months.
I couldn't understand why people from the West weren't writing to me.
They had all my correspondence.
And I learned only later that they assumed that I was giving inform-- they knew there was a leak of information.
And they assumed it was I, because they knew my beliefs and my aversion to communism.
And they thought that this American Joe was the one that I was passing the information to.
So luckily for me, they found out who the culprit was.
It was my boss, and he was shot.
And I was told if that hadn't happened, I would have been sent to Siberia, to the mines, on suspicion only.
So I had a very lucky escape.
And that scared the living daylights out of me.
And after that, I did my utmost to get myself a passport.
Because after the communists took over, normal passports were no longer valid.
And to get back to Britain or to Israel-- I applied for visas to both the places.
It was a question of which came first, which I would sort of honor.
And it took me well over a year before I accrued all the signatures, and permissions, and everything, you know, for me to be able to come to Britain.
And I arrived back here in January, 1949.
That was very hard.
That was incredibly had.
And I hoped she would come, you know, when we settled in marriage, either of us, that she would come and live with us.
But she actually urged me to go.
She had no idea of my involvement, of my political involvement.
I managed to keep her out of that.
I was far too worried, in case you know, it will have-- add further worries to her.
But she went-- she came to see me off.
And I'll never forget standing at the border.
She came-- traveled with me right to the border.
And again, it was snowing so hard.
And she was a lonely figure at the end of the platform, waving.
And it brought back the memory of my parents on the platform, waving.
And I wondered-- I thought, how unfortunate I am to have to flee my country for the second time and, this time, to leave her behind.
Well, she stayed in-- she stayed in her hometown.
And she's lived her normal life.
We were great friends all throughout the three and a half years I was there after the war.
I never lost touch with her.
I visit her every time I go back, every single time.
She's been to stay with me here in Britain.
You can't repay what she did for my parents, and therefore for me.
You can't repay anything like that.
My sister, of course, had stayed, and was, by then, working as a midwife in London.
But I had a bit of a problem before I left, because the Ministry of Defense decided they wanted me to spy for them in England.
And, unfortunately, had I said no, they would never have let me leave, because I knew too much.
So I was trained as a spy.
But of course, I had no intention of working for them.
They wanted me to work on the routes that peop-- the escape routes prominent people were using, you know, to flee from Czechoslovakia to Britain.
And I had to be in London.
And I had to remain single.
And I'm-- so that I would be a free agent, they say, couldn't be help to them.
And they made this perfectly clear.
So obviously, the minute I arrived, I went as far from London as I could to Liverpool and entered Miss [?

Fawkes ?] Secretarial College to learn to be a secretary, so that I could earn my living and again, obviously, was with the Rainfords and my other friends there, though I stayed more or less independent.
But then they started writing to me-- we had a special code with Ministry of Defense-- and threatening me that if I did not play ball, my aunt would suffer.
So you can understand what predicament I was in.
So eventually, I had to go to London, at least please them in that respect.
And I got a job there.
And I kept sending them things which I knew were absolutely useless and unimportant.
And they kept getting so cross.
And they said, you're an intelligent woman.
You know, what you-- what sort of a game are you playing with us?
And I didn't know what to do.
By then, I was going out with a very nice, non-Jewish Englishman, who was a very good friend and desperate to marry me.
And I wasn't all that keen.
But they kept threatening my aunt.
In the end, I got myself pregnant.
And I wrote to them.
And I said, I'm sorry for having a baby.
I've got to get married, which I did, and that I wouldn't be, obviously, any use to them.
And they accepted this.
And I carry that guilt.
I wasn't unhappy in my marriage, but we weren't suited.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, in a sense, I worked at the marriage that much harder because maybe I would have married Michael anyway.
Maybe, but I married him much too quickly and in this particular fashion because I could not bear to risk my aunt being imprisoned after all she'd gone through.
My aunt never knew anything about it.
You know, until then it was-- it all happened within-- I mean, I came in January.
I went for six months to the college.
And in November-- in November of the same year, of '49, I was married.
So I didn't waste anytime.
[LAUGHS] And in may the following year, Clive was born, you know, my son.
So I mean, it was quite an exciting [LAUGHS] time, that post-war time, my sort of introduction to the grownup-- grownup years.
Who was at your wedding?
The Rainfords, my sister, and some friends, you know, and some of my husband's family.
He didn't have a very happy life.
And he was one of seven children.
And the mother left them.
And I so missed my family that when we married, I made our home the sort of core of their existence, because they were scattered all over the place, you know.
And I was very fond of them all.
I still am, so-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but I actually worked for Scotland Yard for two years afterwards.
Because they found out, through their spies, that I was supposed to be working for the Czech government.
They came to interview me.
It was the head of the section for foreigners.
And we hit it off, and he said, would you work for us?
Because you seem-- you seem the right sort.
And I did do that for about a year.
What sort of work did you do?
Interviewing people and finding their political backgrounds, mainly-- sort of unobtrusively.
Well, it was a challenge.
And we needed the money.
And it was good.
I did enjoy it.
I mean, he wanted me to be his secretary after, you know, a full-time job and travel with him.
It would have been great but by then Clive was born.
And I wasn't going to leave my child to these sort of nannies or anything like that.
I didn't want to disrupt my own home life.
I was very happy.
I sort of felt-- I had lost so much in Czechoslovakia, I felt I must put my past behind me, you know, even the Jewish side of it, and just concentrate on being a typical British housewife and mother.
And I tried very hard.
And it wasn't till many years later I realized what a pretence I led.
It wasn't till 1968.
My sister married an English doctor.
And-- but 40 years ago, she emigrated to New Zealand.
Funnily enough, when war ended-- she did such a splendid job with me throughout the war.
I mean, she really encouraged me, and rebuked me, and made sure I was growing up in a way my parents would have approved of.
Because she promised them.
She promised them she would keep an eye on it.
And then they weren't there to see what a splendid job she had done.
And I think for her, the loss of them was even worse than for me.
And it made her a bit sort of-- very sad.
And not that she held it against me, but it was a sort of a wedge between us.
You know, it's very, very hard to explain.
And as the years went-- when she married, and she had a son who's two years younger than my son, we saw each other an awful lot.
But after she went to New Zealand, we only met about four or five times in the last 40 years.
And this will be my first journey to her in all the 40 years.
So obviously, we had kind of grown apart.
When you think of it, that even during the war we only saw each other very briefly during holidays.
And she grew up in a completely different environment than I did, with the Rainfords and the Czech school.
So from the age of 10 and 14 and a half, you know, we were-- we were miles apart in many ways.
So it's a wonder that we've stayed as close as we have.
I mean, I love her dearly.
And she loves me dearly.
That doesn't come into it.
That doesn't alter.
But perhaps we think about things differently and feel about things differently, which, again, is quite normal.
Oh, yes.
Well you saw my-- or you will see my middle daughter, Nicola.
She is going to be 40 next year.
And she is married to a Jamaican and has two beautiful, coffee-colored children whom I absolutely adore.
They are my pride and joy, I might say.
And then I've got my youngest daughter, Sally, who's a very successful fashion accessory designer and importer and has got a terrific name internationally.
She hasn't had an easy life.
She's had health problems.
But she's incredibly strong and incredibly brave and, again, a wonderful daughter of whom I am immensely proud.
But I'm proud of all of them, all three.
My son, as well-- my son, the oldest one is married to an American girl.
And they've got a son.
In fact, my grandchildren range in ages seven, eight and nine-- so very easy to remember.
And they get on very well, of course, being so close to each other.
I see a fair amount of them because Clive lives in Oxfordshire, Nicola in Bristol, Sally in Hampstead.
So though we scattered, we remain very much a family.
Their father died last year.
We have been divorced for quite a few years but we've-- a fond friendship has always remained.
And we've, in fact, been, in many ways, closer since we divorced than we were when we were married.
And we've always had tremendous respect for one another.

I think since 1985, which was 40 years on from the end of the war, when I helped to organize our first school reunion-- I'm talking about the Czech school in Wales, of course-- the realization hit me, and many others, what we all mean to each other.
And so many friendships were renewed and new ones forged and with a little town in Wales, as well.
You know, it's been a godsend to so many of our lives.
It's been absolutely wonderful.
I've got such a wonderful family.
And whether they are in the States, or in Israel, or in the Czech Republic, or here in Britain-- and it doesn't matter if we don't see each other for 40 years or four days.
We know we can rely on one another.
It's amazing feeling, this feeling of knowing you will be understood, knowing you would be helped.
You know, and it really is.
And I think it's because we spent so many extraordinary, extraordinary years together.
And not just that-- I am sharing my life with Harry Steinhauer, with whom I shared a classroom all those years ago.
He's, again, a Czech Jewish child, originally.
But unlike me, his parents and sister managed to follow him.
And it's wonderful you know, having this life we have together, this extended family, and the understanding, which I did not have with my husband, because of our completely different backgrounds.
But more than anything, I got very involved and very much fulfilled when, after 1985, I wrote my autobiography, which is based on the diaries I kept and on all the letters from my parents.

And as I am an author-- I've written books for children and I've worked-- it was a hobby of mine, in translating, also, books from Czech and French into English for various publishers-- I knew that it was a story which was not just mine, but of so many of the children who were here, to a large extent.

And I felt it was my duty to them, and to my parents, to the Jewish race, and to the British people who gave us home, and love, and saved our lives too, and to this wonderful man who saved us, of whom I have said so little so far, and who's a very firm part of my life now, because he only lives down the road.
Nicholas Winton.
He's 87 years old.
And you know, he just took it upon himself to go to Prague and organize the transports.
And he worked tirelessly, on top of his normal job, to find as many homes as possible.
He was a great organizer.

And I mean, he's been honored by the queen for his services to humanity, because he's not only helped us children, he's not only saved our lives and all our children and all the future generations, he's also a founding member of Abbeyfields, the homes for the ages, and has been, for many years, president of [?
Mencap. ?] And he's revered, you know, by so many.
And he's a wonderful, wonderful man.
And so, you know-- and I spend my time now talking, giving talks on my life and on the children transports and on my book.
And each time I give a talk, I feel I am doing something for my parents and for all those who perished.
